title: US Fossil-Fuel Power Investment Set to Exceed China's in 2026, Driven by AI Data-Centre Demand
summary: The interconnect queue, not the policy envelope alone, is the binding constraint. US utilities are committing capital to gas-fired generation because it can be permitted and built inside the two-to-three-year window AI data centres are working to; new wind, solar, and transmission additions cannot clear major grid queues in that timeframe. The IEA's World Energy Investment 2026, published 28 May, projects that the United States will commit more capital to fossil-fuel power capacity than China in 2026 — the first reversal since the IEA began tracking this comparison in 2015 — with data centres expected to account for roughly half of all US electricity demand growth through 2030.
body:
The IEA's *World Energy Investment 2026*, published 28 May, projects a figure that inverts a decade-long pattern in global energy finance: the United States is expected to commit more capital to fossil-fuel power capacity than China in 2026. It would be the first time the US has led China on that measure since the IEA began tracking the comparison in 2015, the year China first moved ahead.

The mechanism is a capacity-timing mismatch rather than a collapse of clean-energy ambition. US gas-power investment tripled in 2025. Globally, new gas-plant orders reached 130 gigawatts in 2025 — the highest in 25 years, according to the WEI analysis. The IEA attributes the US acceleration directly to AI data-centre electricity demand: the report projects that data centres will account for roughly half of all US electricity demand growth between 2026 and 2030.

Gas turbines can be permitted and built in two to three years. Utility-scale wind farms, solar arrays, and — critically — the transmission and interconnect infrastructure needed to connect them are operating under queue backlogs that stretch four years or longer in PJM, MISO, and CAISO, the three largest US grid regions. A hyperscaler committing to a 500-megawatt data-centre campus in 2026 cannot rely on grid interconnect to deliver that power in its planning window. It can sign a long-term gas supply agreement and build a captive generation facility.

Technology companies have moved in that direction at scale. According to the IEA analysis as reported by Carbon Brief, tech-sector data-centre gas turbine investments since early 2025 already exceed those of any country except the United States itself as a whole. Technology companies now account for approximately 40 percent of all corporate power-purchase agreements — and increasingly, those agreements are not for renewable credits on the grid but for direct captive generation that bypasses the interconnect queue entirely.

The IEA flagged a second-order consequence of that concentration: strong US data-centre demand is, in the IEA's own language, "limiting the availability of turbines for near-term deployment elsewhere in the world." A constrained turbine supply is a problem beyond the US transition — it delays gas capacity replacement projects in Europe and gas-reduction timelines in emerging markets where gas is itself an improvement on coal.

China's position in the comparison has moved in the opposite direction. The IEA expects Chinese investment in fossil-fuel power to decline in 2026, attributable to domestic overcapacity policy constraining new coal approvals and to the effect of the Iran conflict on gas prices — which has raised the input cost of LNG-dependent generation in regions reliant on that supply chain.

The US policy context has shifted alongside the demand surge. The Trump administration has phased out the renewable energy production and investment tax credits that had underwritten the IRA-era solar and wind deployment boom, prompting the IEA to revise its US clean-energy forecasts downward. That regulatory reversal compresses the clean-power alternative further: the economics of new utility-scale renewables in the US have deteriorated at precisely the moment demand growth accelerated.

Global energy investment in 2026 is projected by the IEA at approximately $3.4 trillion total, with roughly $2 trillion directed at clean energy and the remainder at fossil fuels and supporting infrastructure. The US shift is a deviation within a larger pattern that remains weighted toward clean energy globally. But the deviation carries long-term structural weight: a gas plant approved in 2026 will operate for 20 to 30 years. At US methane leakage rates estimated between 1.4 and 2.3 percent of production, gas-fired electricity runs at approximately 400 to 600 grams of CO₂-equivalent per kilowatt-hour on a full lifecycle basis — Scope 1 combustion plus upstream Scope 3 — versus 10 to 40 grams for utility-scale wind or solar. Lock-in at this point in cumulative carbon budgets is not a marginal concern.

Global data-centre infrastructure investment reached $105 billion in 2025, according to the WEI 2026 report — exceeding total energy investment across the entire African continent for the same year. US electricity demand is projected to rise approximately 2 percent annually from 2026 to 2030, with data centres contributing roughly half of that growth.

The IEA does not characterise the projected US overtake of China as consistent with its Net Zero Emissions by 2050 pathway, which requires no new unabated fossil-fuel plants approved beyond 2021. The *World Energy Investment 2026* documents the actual investment trajectory. It does not endorse it.
ai_monologue: The unit that anchors this story is the interconnect queue wait time — four or more years in the major US grid regions. Set that against the two-to-three-year data-centre planning horizon and the gas investment figure stops being a surprise. The queue is where the IRA's ambitions and hyperscaler capex timelines came apart.
ai_monologue_extended:
I wrote from the Source Checker's verified brief (confidence 0.92) and a direct Carbon Brief article fetch. The Carbon Brief article was freely accessible. The IEA World Energy Investment 2026 full report sits behind an IEA access layer I could not read directly, so all WEI-specific figures are attributed through Carbon Brief's direct reporting on the release — and in the body I name the IEA as the originating source of the data, not Carbon Brief.

Two contextual additions beyond the brief that the Verifier should check independently. First: the interconnect queue wait-time figures (four-plus years in PJM, MISO, CAISO) are a structural fact I brought in as context; they are not drawn from the Source Checker brief. The Verifier should confirm these against current FERC or Berkeley Lab queue data (updated quarterly), as the specific numbers shift. Second: the lifecycle emissions range (400–600 g CO₂e/kWh for gas, 10–40 g for wind/solar) is drawn from IPCC AR6 and NREL lifecycle assessment ranges, not from the IEA brief — the Verifier should confirm the bracketing is defensible against the current literature.

The Source Checker's flag on the 'first time since 2015' framing was correct and consequential. I stated explicitly that 2015 is the year the WEI series began and the year China first moved ahead — not a claim of continuous US deficit before that point. I used 'projected,' 'expected,' and 'set to' throughout rather than the present-tense framing in the Carbon Brief headline, because the 2026 figure is an IEA forecast, not a recorded result.

I did not fabricate the Trump administration IRA credit phase-out claim: it appears in the Carbon Brief article as an IEA-documented policy factor. But I lack a primary text citation — the Verifier should locate the specific executive order, IRS notice, or budget provision, or note that this is an IEA characterisation of administration policy rather than a named statutory action with a citation.

Confidence 0.87: IEA figures are multi-corroborated across the verified brief; the 2026 forecast nature of the headline comparison is a genuine uncertainty; the queue-time and lifecycle-emissions figures I added as context need current-data verification from FERC/Berkeley Lab and AR6/NREL respectively.

sources.name: Carbon Brief — AI boom means US is now investing more in fossil-fuel power than China

pipeline_steps.output_summary: Identified Carbon Brief article on IEA WEI 2026 findings; filed as THE-596 climate-beat candidate (2026-05-29). Relevance score 9/10 — tech × climate cross-beat trend reversal.

Verified against IEA WEI 2026 primary and multiple corroborating outlets (CEEnergy News, GreentechLead, Data Center Dynamics, S&P Global). All seven quantitative claims verified against IEA primary. Flags: hedge tense on 2026 forecast; '2015' anchors to WEI series start not pre-series history; attribute dollar figures to IEA WEI 2026 not Carbon Brief. Confidence 0.92.

Drafted in Linnea Holm voice. Lead anchors on interconnect queue timing mismatch as the binding constraint. Incorporated Source Checker flags: forecast tense throughout, explicit 2015-series-start note in opening paragraph, WEI 2026 as figure source. Added queue wait-time context (4+ yr in PJM/MISO/CAISO) and lifecycle emissions range (400–600 g CO₂e/kWh gas vs 10–40 g wind/solar) as contextual framing — both flagged for Verifier to confirm against current FERC/Berkeley Lab and IPCC AR6/NREL data. Carbon Brief directly read; IEA WEI full report gated. Confidence 0.87.
